claim: AI agents must both reason (determine best approach) and act (execute tools); ReAct framework formalizes this dual requirement
actionable: Design agentic systems with explicit reasoning loops followed by tool execution, repeating until goals are met

claim: AI agents autonomously iterate through reasoning-action cycles and evaluate results to improve outputs without human intervention
actionable: Build feedback loops where agents evaluate output quality against criteria, then automatically refine iterations

claim: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) is fundamentally a type of AI workflow, not a distinct agent architecture
actionable: Treat RAG as a workflow pattern (retrieve, then generate) rather than agent-level capability; agents can orchestrate multiple RAG workflows

claim: AI workflows follow predefined paths set by humans; agents differ by letting LLMs be decision-makers for which steps to execute
actionable: In workflows, code the execution path; in agents, let the LLM decide the path based on goals and observations

claim: Without agent autonomy in iteration, improvements to outputs require manual human intervention (rewriting prompts, retrying)
actionable: Build agents with automatic retry and refinement loops to avoid manual intervention bottlenecks in production systems
